item.title: Per-Tool Sandboxing for AI Agents: Why One Sandbox Is Not Enough
item.description: Enterprise Linux Kernel Solutions for optimal performance and reliability.

item.title: the 90 day disclosure policy is dead
item.description: TLDR The 90 day responsible disclosure window was built for a world where bug finders were rare and exploit development was slow. That world is gone. LLMs have compressed both timelines to near-zero. I have seen it first hand, and so has everyone else paying attention. This post lays out why the old model is broken, with real stories, and makes one ask to the industry: treat every critical security issue as P0 and patch it immediately.

item.title: Three Cultures of Math
item.description: Recent advances in general models — ChatGPT and Claude — have started to autonomously solve open mathematical problems. For example, Erdős 1196, Tim Gowers’s PhD student problems, OpenAI’s Ramsey numbers result. There are a lot of caveats — were the problems widely enough studied, could the solutions be coming from some past human result, etc. But if one zooms out and considers the progression of AI capabilities, it is hard not to conclude that more and more open problems will be solvable by autonomous AI.

item.title: Europe Is Losing Its Best Engineers — Not to Emigration, but to Management
item.description: One of the things we didn't expect when building CNTR was a pattern we kept seeing in CVs. Incredible technical or academic career starts, strong first steps — and then a shift into management roles. Followed by the loss of the technical edge. We saw it again and again.

item.title: Froot Loops and a graphics card · Daniel May
item.description: On a 2006 email from my dad's colleague, the parser I wrote in response, and what I got paid in.

item.title: Freediving, Embodiment and Humanity
item.description: In this post I'd like to utilize my experience with freediving as an excuse to talk about a bunch of seemingly contrasting qualities and emotions, which together — I feel — illustrate some deeper aspects of experiencing humanity. For me, at least, freediving has turned out a surprisingly useful tool in this

item.title: Piloting Agentic Engineering - What Software Engineers Can Learn From The Aviation Industry • Maximilian Walterskirchen
item.description: AI coding agents are amazing, but lean on them too hard and your engineering skills atrophy. Aviation already lived through this. Here's what we can steal from how they fixed it.

item.title: Incident Report: CVE-2024-YIKES
item.description: A series of unfortunate events.

item.title: Inspecting My Balcony Battery's Cloud Telemetry | Luca Becker
item.description: A tcpdump capture led to a closer look at the Marstek B2500-D cloud endpoint: plain HTTP, AES-128-ECB, and a static key.
item.author: (Luca Becker)

item.title: extremely low frequencies
item.description: The submarine is a surprisingly ancient technology—at least in its early, primitive forms. The idea is quite simple, that a well-enough-sealed boat ought to be able to submerge and resurface. It's the practicalities that make the whole thing difficult. It is generally considered that the US Civil War was the first use of submarines in combat; these were primitive machines with very limited operating endurance and navigational capabilities. These submarines were more like torpedoes: you pointed them in the right direction and hoped they went straight.

item.title: The Cost of Overfitting the Harness
item.description: OpenAI winding down fine tuning is an interesting development and one to watch.

item.title: The Struggle is Gone
item.description: When I was 18 I started studying physics at University and got my first real taste of “the struggle”.

item.title: Wonky Software Supply Chains - Simon Ramstedt
item.description: Simon Ramstedt, 2026-04-09 · last updated 2026-04-24

item.title: Summer of PLT
item.description: Looking for a summer coding project? Try making a programming language!

item.title: Detecting PostgreSQL optimization issues with deterministic analysis
item.description: Detecting PostgreSQL optimization issues with deterministic analysis For years, I kept seeing the same PostgreSQL problems in production: missing foreign key indexes stale statistics unused indexes datatype mismatches sequences approaching exhaustion postgres configuration Most of these issues are not difficult to detect. They already exist inside PostgreSQL catalogs, statistics, … The difficult part is usually connecting the signals together and understanding their operational impact. For the past months, I have been working on a deterministic PostgreSQL analysis approach inside pgAssistant 2.8.

item.title: The Worlds Left To Conquer — Ludicity
item.description: It has been a year and a half since I quit my job to start a consultancy. It took me years to build up to quitting, and I had not only a chip on my shoulder, but to quote Seth Sentry, “the guac and the dip and the salsa.” The people that read this blog probably understand what I’m talking about. I looked around at how organizations are run, at the people that told me what to do, and thought “Surely I could do a better job than this.”

item.title: The death of the roadmap — Readables
item.description: The 12-month engineering plan I built six months ago is now irrelevant. Notes on what changes when leadership has to be fullstack.

item.title: Your Scrum Isn't Scrum. The Scrum Guide Is 13 Pages. Your Process Has 300.
item.description: User stories, epics, story points, planning poker, velocity, Fibonacci — none of it is in the Scrum Guide. Ten biggest myths and what to do instead.
item.author: (Nicolas Bouvrette)

item.title: Local AI Needs to be the Norm
item.description: Local AI models should be the default.

item.title: Agent Readiness
item.description: Most teams use agents as glorified autocomplete. A framework for assessing and raising both codebase readiness and team adoption — the two dimensions that have to move together.

item.title: Application performance is a product requirement | Christian Rackerseder
item.description: Application performance is not something engineers can optimize in isolation. It is a product decision, a requirement and a trade-off that has to be made explicit.

item.title: COBOL Is the Asbestos of Programming Languages
item.description: The most widely adopted computer language in history, COBOL is now causing a host of problems. It's also dangerously difficult to remove.
item.author: (Zeb Larson)

item.description: Stephen Brennan's personal website and blog.

item.description: Bambu Labs controversy heats up.
item.author: (https://www.tomshardware.com/author/denise-bertacchi)

item.title: GrapheneOS (@GrapheneOS@grapheneos.social)
item.description: Apple and Google are gradually expanding their use of hardware-based attestation. They're convincing a growing number of services to adopt it. Google's Play Integrity API and Apple's App Attest API are very similar. Apple brought it to the web via Privacy Pass, which Google intends on doing too.

item.title: HomeGrown Devlog: Programming the Research System
item.description:
Wishlist HomeGrown on Steam: https://store.steampowered.com/app/1894560/HomeGrown/
Play my previous game "Equilinox": https://store.steampowered.com/app/853550/Equilinox/
Support the channel on Patreon and get access to the game & code for Homegrown, the city-builder, and Equilinox: https://www.patreon.com/thinmatrix
Follow the game's progress on my social media: Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/thinmatrix/ Twitter: https://twitter.com/ThinMatrix Bluesky: https://bsky.app/profile/thinmatrix.bsky.social Trello: https://trello.com/b/W3zkIJTM/farm Email: thinmatrix@gmail.com
Background music by Jamal Green: https://open.spotify.com/artist/50jTMgIPZrjKFgHbCvoeRt?si=Z-xfUXEXQCuGdGRy7qf9Ug&dl_branch=1
#devlog #HomeGrown
item.author: (ThinMatrix)

item.title: France Moves to Break Encrypted Messaging
item.description: France's parliament just endorsed the one idea every cryptographer on earth has already debunked and they're calling it a compromise.

item.title: bits from the release team
The left-wing case for AI
item.description: In Many anti-AI arguments are conservative arguments I argued that left-wing anti-AI sentiment1 is partly a backlash to two unrelated events around the rise of ChatGPT: the crypto mania of 2022 and the pro-Donald-Trump push many big tech CEOs made in 2024. If the timing had been different, we could have had a real pro-AI faction on the left. What would that look like?

item.title: GitHub - imtomt/ymawky: MacOS Web Server written entirely in ARM64 assembly
item.description: MacOS Web Server written entirely in ARM64 assembly - imtomt/ymawky

item.title: You should read Programming as Theory Building
item.description: When I finished reading Peter Naur’s Programming as Theory Building my first thought was “How come nobody ever told me to read this?” I ended up reading it multiple times, as I attempted to collect my thoughts on why it makes so much sense.

item.title: Apple is increasing my cortisol levels
item.description: My blog, where I attempt to collect my thoughts and share the occasional interesting topic with others

item.title: I’ve banned query strings — Chris Morgan
item.description: 🗓️ 2026-05-08 • Tagged /web, /opinions, /meta=only

item.title: The Intolerable Hypocrisy of Cyberlibertarianism
item.description: I like the Internet. I am old enough to remember the pre-Internet era and despite the younger generations pining for those simpler days, I was there. Paper maps were absolutely horrible, just you and a compass in your car on the side of the road in the middle of the
